speaker: Laura Schrager
words: On this tape, Alfred Erickson recalls his parents' arrival at hog meadows from Minnesota in the early homesteaders in the area. He describes their first home, the Janesville Post Office's moves, the naming of hog meadows, hardships in the early years and killing a bear. His two sisters, Lena Erickson Justice and Mae Erickson -Lamar, mention some of their experiences helping around the home and farm and flunking at the logging camps.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: My folks come from Minnesota in the year of 1892.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: They come out because my mother had sent out money to her nephew to buy this place, which belonged to a fellow by the name of Con. I don't remember his first name. Con was a Civil War veteran, and he didn't care to stay back there in that snow belt. He wanted to get out of there, so he said he wanted to sell. Well, John Berg, my mother's nephew, had a place right beside the hog meadows on the east side of the hog meadows. John Berg's place. Well, he made the deal. My dad didn't have a cent in that property. It was all my mother's money.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Anyway,

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: the old man, he was just about to leave Moore at the station. No, on the train. He was about to jump off of the train after the train got started, coming out west.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Moore had to talk to him and get him kind of cooled down. So she finally got him cooled down enough that he went and sat down in the seat and sat there to try. I didn't know that.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, well, of course, I wasn't born yet. At that time, I was born 1893. They'd been there a year.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then they had to—the house that I was born in, it was sitting way over on the north side and on the west side, northwest side of the place. They had a fireplace in the back. I remember that log house. They had a fireplace just like that right over there. All the road that was made out of rock.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then they had their big room here. This is the big end. Then in between, the next end was the barn. They had a place there big enough for two horses and a couple of cows. Then they had a haymow right up over. Well, they used this middle place to pile up wood in to keep it dry. It was built something like what they had up at Beauvel. They had a warns built on that meadow up there. It was a building here, you know, log house. Then over here, another log house.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then they had the full roof over the both places, don't you see?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So it made it pretty nice. Well, anyway, they only lived there about three or four years. Then they decided they were going to build a house. So they went to work and they built a house for folks. That is my dad got help, you know. Then they built a house. After the house was built, we moved down there. Well, the place up where I was born, that's set there to let practically run it down. Well, the old man tore the barn down because that stuck right out in the field. We had a field right up on that side of the hill there. So he tore that down so he'd make a bigger field.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Just about where the pipe went there, you see? Remember we used to have the white pipe plants?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. God, we used to go. There were some wonderful pipe plants there. That roomworm, you know. Well, anyway, there was a lot of work there. There was no fence. Con didn't have no cattle. He didn't have no horses. So he just let the sheep men run over his place. They didn't give him nothing, but he didn't care.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: He figured he would stay in there until he proved up. But then he got disgusted and thought he would just sell out and get $500. That's what the folks put up, and that was too much money.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How many acres was that they got?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, 136 acres. There was a fraction in that place there.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: That's what my dad was—he was awfully disappointed. Well, I couldn't blame him. Going into a wood country like that there, just timber, you know, and hard winters. You had to go out and put in here every day, or you wouldn't be snowed in. Trump wrote. Well, anyway, he finally got a fence made around the place there, and then he got to plowing up a little of the meadow. I put it into Timothy, Clover. We used to raise some wonderful wild hay there. Gee, it was a beautiful wild hay.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Then the old man got so that well done, he built a big barn. He built a big barn that'll hold about 35, 40 tons of hay. The hay was always saleable in the wintertime there. A lot of fellas around had a lot of cattle. So the old man could always sell a bunch of hay there. He never was stuck with it. So he made pretty good on the hay. Then he did a little work on the Cowdy Road. He worked on that, but in later years it becomes a state road, don't you know, state highway. That was another pretty good thing. He usually gets in about three months work in the summertime on the roads, what they call the County Roads, built by the county. Well, my dad was the one that helped build the road into Collins, not into Collins, but into the Warren Meadow, the Bowe Vel Meadows. Now, the road used to go what they call around the Horse Ranch. That goes south of Bowe Vel, and then it goes to the Forks of the Potlatch.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: You know where the Forks of the Potlatch is?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, at the Forks of the Potlatch was a family living there by the name of Carlson's. Carlson was the first man to run the post office up there, and he named it Janesville. That was established in Janesville. Well, he run that post office for several years. It made it further for my folks to go, because it was quite a ways over there to the Forks of the Potlatch.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was there anything else to Janesville besides that post office?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, they didn't have a store there, but I'll get around to that. Then after a couple of years, the post office was changed to Hog Meadows to Hainsley. Hainsley owned that main part of the meadow, and he built a nice big house there. He had the biggest barn there, too. Well, then he decided that he would build on a little store building right next to the house on the Hog Meadows there.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Make it look like a store, don't you know? You know how they used to make them fronts on the stores there?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, he wanted to start in a little grocery store there, which he did, and got the post office. Well, then that was a little money coming in there all the time. Then he put in a few groceries. Well, there were a lot of them on homesteaders around in there. Well, they didn't have so far to go for their groceries, you see. That made it nice. So then Hainsley had that till the time he got ready to sell and sell out. When it was to be sold out, then Lawrence, G .R. Lawrence, on the other side of Helmer, he wanted to get the James Rule post office. So they moved it from there to Russ Lawrence's place. And then Russ Lawrence built a store, too. So he got in a bunch of groceries, and he'd done pretty good in the solar business there. And that's the way it went in the last place. That's where James Rule wound up. Well, it wound up with Lawrence's. Then they changed the post office, and then they put it up at Helmer. There is word that moved the post office, but then it was changed to the name of Helmer, Helmer post office. Jamesville was done away with down there at Lawrence's.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So Jamesville actually moved three times, twice.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, now that's once. It moved over there, once. Two Marshals, twice. To Jamesville at Lawrence's, it was three times. Three times it moved.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: But I don't remember, oh, I remember when the old man would drive down there to Jamesville there to Hawke Medicine, get the mail. I remember that. But outside of that, I...

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: But the people now, there wouldn't be no use of mentioning the people living there on these places, would it?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You mean like you did in the car?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why don't you... I think so. I think so. You don't have to tell, you know, all the detail, but just mention the guys that were the first homesteaders in Hawke Meadows.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: What little bit, what you know about them.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Shouldn't you go along as the kids come along, like the folks? No, that wouldn't be necessary, would it? What do you mean? What were the children we had?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: The children that were being born. I was thinking of the homesteaders that actually came there before. Yeah, but I mean, brothers and sisters he has and... Oh, yeah. Yeah. But I thought men would go back to that first and then we should get into the family. Oh, I see. Okay. But the people that were living around there that was shopping and getting their mail and that, James Wellman over at the other place, Helmer and then Lawrence says, the people that lived around there that... Yeah. All of the people at homesteaders didn't come in there at that time, when they came in.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Right?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, just around Hawke Meadows there. That's right. Yeah, well now that the Hawke Meadows, I was telling you, is that there was three people living there. There was Hainsley, there was Marshall, there was Hopkins, there was the three that lived on the Hawke Meadows. And John Berg, my cousin, he was on the east end of the meadows. That's how...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Like if you come from Helmer up through Hawke Meadows, don't you know?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, you know that's a pretty long big meadow there. Well, it's right up in that east corner is where my cousin homesteaded, John Berg.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Now, I don't think there would be... Well, I forgot the name, some of those living way back in the woods. And the other people that lived right around where we lived there was... Well, to begin with...

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Was Jim Kelly. He was on the north end. Jim Kelly. Austin Justice. He was on the north side too, but on the west.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Now, I don't know about the King Meadows, now the claim there was a fellow by the name of a king that homesteaded down the King Meadows, you know. I don't know if that's true and I don't know if anybody knows anything about that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How about Acres?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, Acres.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He come in last there, don't you know?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: He come in there last. Well, there's one, two, three. And Tom Shane, did he... Well, Tom Shane didn't own no land. He just rented it. Yeah, well, no, no, Theodore Foll was the owner. Theodore Foll. And Theodore Foll bought it from...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Cook. Theodore Foll bought it from Cook. That's the man that owned the Shea Meadows.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: And no Shea was there for about four, five, six years and rented from Foll. Foll was the owner.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: And our place was the only place up there that never had a mortgage. Never had a mortgage on our place. All the other places had mortgages except for Day Cream.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How about the Bronson's?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, Bronson was owned by Bronson there, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: His place, you remember that fence up there at the Bronson there as you're coming out onto the meadow?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes. Well, that was the line right there of Bronson at Homestead there, you know. He come clear down to the old man, joining the old man, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you know how Hog Meadows got its name?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: A fellow named Nichols lived now and I see in this book. The Galloway brothers was the ones who brought the big herd of hogs in there to root the cams out of the ground there on that Hog Meadows. But my dad said that a fellow by the name of Nichols, and he lived down on Texas Ridge, he was the fellow that brought the hogs in there. So you see there, that's mixed up too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And that's how it got its name because he brought in a big herd of hogs?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Yeah, he had, oh, he put two or three hundred head hogs up there, you know, and let them summer up there, don't you know, and work on that. Why, God, you know that... I like that camas. That camas grew just like an onion, you know. Oh, God, it grew that big. And it's pretty good to eat stuff. That's the Indians lived on that, you know, they made bread out of that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They eat the root and the fruit too, right?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Was there some kind of a fruit that grew... Well, they grow like an onion. No, they grow like an onion. They had to grow on top, you know, on it. The prettiest flower you ever seen, you know, purple -blue. And that was sure fine. We'll have to get out and get some camas. I wish that camas was there, my gosh, I'd go and root out some.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you eat that a lot as kids?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I've tasted it, but it's kind of... It's kind of greasy. Well, it is kind of a gummy -like. It wasn't what you call goods to eat. But then if a person knew how to fix that, the Indians knew how to fix it, how to cook it. They baked it. And they made good bread, they claim.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you see Indians up around here when you were young?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: We used to camp on our folks' place early in the morning to be out there digging camas. Dad had to change them all many, many times.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would your kids go out and play with them at all? You know, go out and see the Indians, or were you not allowed?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yeah, we'd go and see them. They even offered us something to eat. I don't remember that before my time, I guess. No, you were. The only thing I remember about Indians, they'd come to our place and get water from our well. They'd have one of these canvas sacks to fill Latin and get water, and then they would go. I have a memory. When I was young, I remember sitting in an apple box on a table. My grandmother was out milking the cows. She'd come in with two buckets of milk, and you could see her trotting from way down the window there, up in the house, and be snow that deep. Then the first thing she'd do was strain some milk and give me a big glass of warm milk to drink.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Out in the north of them. God, you sure have a lot of good time on your hands. You got two watches. Yeah. I'd better load you one.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, that's about all I know to tell about the thing out there. Go on to the bow vale. We're pretty famous people. We're pretty famous. They started to store up there. Yeah. Bow vale, of course, Warren's did not have no store. They didn't even have a post office there. So, of course, bow vale, they were from rich people. They were from high society in England. You know, Mrs. Bow vale was related to the King of England.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: You've seen the picture of her, didn't you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, I guess they were Lord. Was he a...

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: What was he, a Count?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: He was something. A remittance man. I think he was a remittance man. I should have brought my book along, I guess, for reference about the bow vales.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember them personally very much? Who? Do you remember them personally? The bow vales?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yeah, because we traded there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You'd go up there and trade?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yes. We traded there quite a bit.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of store did they have?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Grocery.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Some hard... not hard work, but dry goods, such as men's clothing, shoes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Yardage?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Teamwork. Well, bow vale was quite a place at one time, too. Well, sure, after the town, after they started the town, then, of course, it built up to be quite a bit.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So we thought we wouldn't think about it, would you? You been there yet? But it's god -dang dead looking now, don't you know it?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Town of Overless Fright.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, you can imagine the amount of people that's working there for the Potlary -Slum -a Company, you know, there's over 1 ,200 to 1 ,500 men there a year around. So you may know that there was quite a payroll around there. But now, God a man has starved to death there now.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was you in any of the places when you was up there, Bill? Did you go in the taverns?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Let's see, there's two taverns saying, no, there's three. There's several there, just two, I don't remember. There's three.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do you remember the town going in there, or were you out on the homestead most of the time?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, we stayed on the homestead there, you know, till, well, when I got old enough, I'd go out and go to work somewhere, you know, too. I even worked in the logging camps. I went into the logging camps, a flunking one time at the age of about, oh, 17, 18 years. 18 years of age.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, let's go back to more when you were kids growing up on the homestead. You know, if you could tell me things about what it was like living there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And how many more sisters and brothers were there?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: There was three girls and... Two boys. Four girls. Four girls and two boys.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I'm the only one left.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Of the boys.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I think I'm doing pretty good for an old devil.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: You wouldn't have been around this long if you hadn't been a devil. Thank you.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: One thing, the winters were severe. You'd be snowed in in the winter, except when you go through and break the road, take a team of horses and break the road through to the main road. The winters were long and severe.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Could you get out to go to school along the winter?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Our school was interrupted a lot due to weather conditions. Well, you know, May and Molly both went out and boarded someplace. Sometimes they worked for the board. Worked for room and board, and that's where I got most of my education. I'm a god. I think I named the months that I've gone, I guess.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: But there's very little schooling we ever got.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How far away was the school?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, the three miles. They called it three miles to that, it was the lower end of the hog meadows. Yeah, but when I started school...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You started Helmer, didn't you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I started Helmer.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And May?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: We started in Derry, I started in Derry. But we had to rent an apartment and remain away from home. That was during the First World War when my brother was in service. It was really a tough, it was tough food, I'm telling you. That dang right. They had to wrap their feet with gunny sacks. That's a good joke. I saw my brother do that with my younger brother, especially. They had to put gunny sacks to keep the toes from freezing off. I got it with terrible cold. No, we didn't do it in Derry, but I've seen that at home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They'd have to put them in the closet at home, no?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Wrap their feet in gunny sacks and pour water on them and let it freeze so they keep them from freezing to death.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you really do that?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Sure. We'd have to go feed the stock. We didn't have any rubber, so we put them outside of our shoes, gunny sacks. And when they wore out underneath, we'd turn them over and we'd... Turn them over and put them inside. People think they have it hard nowadays. Well, you had to go dress like an Eskimo.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We didn't have things at rest in warm, we just asked them, was that fair?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: We didn't have buffalo hide or anything like that. I should say not. Money was hard to get. Taxes always had to be paid and the most had to have feet for our stock. See, that was the two things that you had to do.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What year was that made when he was in the service?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: That would be in 1918.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: We run out of hay. I had quite a few head of cattle and that's our brother. He bought some hay from up at Shamed, it was there, from Pete Asculpt.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was it, $36 a ton?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: It seemed like that's what it was. $30 a ton, unless you wanted... Oh, it was terrific. Anyway, we had an awful time getting that hay home. It was breaking in the spring and all the horses go through and when it starts to rain and the snow was kind of crusty. Now you need the water to be running and then the horses would break through this snow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that a particularly hard winter?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, I think it was, really. We had not a lot of snow. Yeah, there was some time when the winters were not so hard and other times the God Almighty is now. I think we measured that on the flat, it was in the 30th year, it was way up like that. It was higher in my head anyway. We dug clear down to the ground to see how deep it was.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So our stock come off kind of thin in the spring. Yes, they did. Well, let's go up and listen to our Elbert's Cook. No, wait a minute, we've got to finish this. You haven't told the story about the children.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: About what?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: About us kids. Well, I don't see anything new about it. Oh, it's old, that's why it's interesting. She wants to know about that. That's the part she comes on with. Well, we have to know what's the number, I guess, and that would be always just six of us children in the family. We'll have to.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, also, just what it was like, how your parents got by, what kind of things you kids had to do to help out on the homestead.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Why, sure. Sure. Tell them about it. Tell them about how you had to help out. Oh, God, in the morning, you're in the wintertime, well, you had to get out and feed.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And how many of you had done this?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, I and Emil, when he got big enough, I and Emil and the old man, we'd all go out there. And then we'd have to cut wood. Jiminy Christmas, you know, you had to cut, we had two stoves that are gone, usually the cook stove and the heater. And you're using all the lot of wood in the country like that. And the water that we carry from our well. Yeah, we did. We had a well about. We had the water in the payload. We'd bring in like the night before it would freeze overnight in the water bucket, right in the kitchen. Yeah. That's how cold it was. You could hear those balsam trees popping out there, snapping and popping. You know, it was cold when they start to snap. You're right. Balsam will do that at first, I guess, when it gets to a certain temperature. Well, the wagon within five miles, you could hear, Greer, Greer, Greer, Greer, Greer. You could hear the cattlemen, they'd be walking when they'd be feeding, you know, at night, you could hear when they walk on that snow, it just makes that funny noise. It'd be so cold. It makes snow powdery. Yeah, that's breaching the wound.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And what was the girls doing on when you were doing this now?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Who was... Well, you know, they were pretty small, you know. May was a baby.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Molly... Well, anyway then, the oldest one, tell her about the oldest one, and then the next oldest, and the girls and whatnot. Well, you know, my older sister, she worked out quite a bit. So, Dad left Molly with quite a bit of work. May was the baby, of course. But as she grew up, you know, she was quite a bit of help there. They really were good to help there in the kitchen. I was better out in the field, though. I'd rather work outside.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of work would you do outside?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Fitchin' hay. Soggin' hay in May. Oh, yes, I've done that. We've loaded. When we were during the war, I used to load hay, and you kids would pitch it on for me. Yeah, May would be up there loading. One time she went overboard between the horses.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Remember that thing?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I stepped out too far. That's a good thing they were in almost any...

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Haven't you been born on a farm?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No. No. Oh, well, then you don't know nothing about it. But, God, you're sure in your time you must have seen a lot of those kids on the farm workin'. They don't work now the way they used to. Some things are the same, but a lot. So much has changed. Mostly machinery and all that. Tell about how many livestock you had, and the sheep you had to run. Well, we run... We used to have about 20 head of cattle. 20 and 25 had of cattle, and about 15, 20 had of sheep. Two or three hogs. And probably a couple of dozen chickens, and what we used to have, but cut two dozen. Yeah, I imagine. But I think we had more cattle than that, though. Seemed like we run... Well, we did at one time up to about 30 head. Fifty, I think. Oh, no, no, no. I think we had about the time when you sold them off. Remember, you decided to quit the cattle bed, and you had built it up to about 50 head. We had a pretty good blood of stonk.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How many horses did you have to work with?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, we only had two. Then you had another coyose.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where would you run those cattle?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: That's a lot of cattle. Well, God, we had that whole territory up there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The neighbors now, there is three or four neighbors, didn't have any cattle, don't you see? Well, our cattle could run on their ground, don't you see?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: We could have run... If we could have raised enough hay, we would have run a lot more cattle in there. But then we just had to go according to what hay we could harvest.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: It wasn't a good paying proposition because it cost too much.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What cost too much?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, if you had to buy hay and haul it in there, it really cost quite a bit. So we couldn't see any profit much in it. You couldn't raise enough hay you had there to make it worthwhile. That's it. That would be a timber to their income. You see, there was only about, well, there wasn't quite 40 acres in farmland. You've been up there. Well, it's only about 40 acres.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Wouldn't 40 acres hardly have farmland? Where would you sell the cattle?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Why, buyers had come in there. Buyers come in from Moscow, from Poland, and other places. They come in to buy up fat cattle. And then we butchered a lot. Sold the meat to the companies, lumber companies.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you do the same with the sheep? Did they just run loose?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: They just run loose, yeah. They're not the neighbor bothersome. Oh, a coyote once in a while would kill one. But then that was to be expected, you know. They just took that as a natural loss. Yep. A bear or something like that would get in there and they'd run loose. Kind of make a killing once in a while. We'd share the sheep in the spring and sell the wool to a local merchant. They have several sacks of wool. I remember sheep sharing time very well. Remember, we got that fancy machine sharing sheep. Yeah. Alford, he had been down to Hooper and he learned how to share the sheep with a machine. So he decided to buy one, so we had to run this little crank, you know. It made it quite a bit more difficult. Oh, that was a pretty good dang good little sharing machine. Yeah, it was. He just sent the sheep and he'd share them around there and we'd be cranking away on that little thing. Remember, you'd get tired too. Oh, yeah, we'd have to change. Yeah, we'd get the count on it. And then, of course, we'd have to take the little scissors and clip her on their ears, eyes and face. We're doing barber deal there. Yeah, we learned barbering. No, I shared for the people around.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: With that machine?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yep.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I went up and shared the crane sheep one time. He had about 30 head up there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Old crane, you remember? Who turned the crank for you then? Old crane?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, and the boys. Them two boys, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of machine was that? Was it a lot better than that?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, it was, I set off God Ascent to Chicago.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: And I don't remember what it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: It was $12 or something, you think?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, it must have been more than that. It was a pretty good machine. Looks like $12 sticks in my eye. I got an old Sears and Roebuck catalog down there chosen in it. I bet it does have it in it. Oh, yeah. If you got the old time. I think they were about $12. If you had little clippers on it, it looked like hand clippers that you'd use for cutting hair. You know, all my children share over here. That's not true. Anybody's. And it worked somewhat on that order.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I remember, right?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I had a little cry. I had the elbows on it. I can show you one down there. It was a reflexive shaft. I think that's how much you talk about it. Well, it's on the same principle as clippers that they use on humans, you know. The barbers use. That's what I say. It's about the same. Just about the same. That knife goes just like that. There weren't many people that had the same knife.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ever get a deer once in a while?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Yeah. Emel was the best hunter. I never could get an elk. I killed a bear one time, and I killed a deer. Emel was a god -dang -good hunter. He could get the deer and the elk. And I couldn't get an elk to save my soul. He looked like an Indian, and he knew the tricks. And he used to trap, too, as my youngest brother. He used to trap weasels and martin and... Kyle, in the winter, you know, for extra money. He knew how to prepare the pelts, and he'd send them in to St. Louis, I think.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Taylor, hiding for a company wasn't it?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I don't know what I remember. He'd catch a lot of weasels, and they do have a beautiful pelt. He always had trap line going in the winter.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would he have to go far? Would he go far to trap?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, he'd only have a little bit of trap.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh, yeah, for a mile sometime? A mile?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: For a mile, maybe. He'd go up on the... around, not those creeks, but...

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Brownstone Meadows? Didn't he ask them up along that creek, I think?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Through the little creeks that flew through the meadow.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You killed a bear, did you eat the meat?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No. No. My God, I didn't eat the meat, and all, though I have eaten bear meat, my gosh. But I don't know. I killed a pretty as bear. God, he was fat. He was yearling.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I can't understand the taste of it. It tastes like pork. I taste it. Oh, it tastes awful good. You've got to know how to... You've got to know how to prepare that stuff. You've got to parboil it. You have to get a parboil, then you put it in that roasting pan, and put it into the oven, and then roast it. Then you really got good meat. It's a fact.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where did you kill that bear?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, pretty close to where we lived.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Brown Bear, by God, you know, I was... I'd been to town, and I stayed all night in town, to Bow Bell. So on my way back home, I'd got within a whole half a mile of our place. I run onto a bear, and he was a -feeding along the road. This brush has got these white berries on.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You've seen that kind of brush, haven't you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: It's called winter berries. Great clusters of that, white berries. And I got this close to him, as far as some hair to the wall over there, maybe a little closer, yeah, a little closer than that. He popped out of the brush and into the road. And he faced me. And I thought, well, my God, he looks ugly. And the longer I stood there and looked at him, the worse he was. God, the hair come up, you know, with the wolves up on his... from his shoulders, clear to the back of his head. I seen he meant fight. I didn't dare try and get him out of the road. So then there was a little ditch alongside the road, and so I thought, well, I'm going to cross this ditch and go on the other side, on the side of the hill there. It wasn't too far to the road over there, where I could go running, getting on the road, and go on up the hill. So I went on around him. A bear, he watched. He just stood there and watched, right along, until I was clear out of sight. When I got home, well, I told him, well, I said, by golly, I think we can get a bear this morning.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He says, where?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: And I told him. So I said, let's get the gun and go. So we got the gun, we walked back. And the bear hadn't traveled, I don't think, any longer further. Well, maybe about twice as long as from here to that wall back there. And he was sent to the bend of the road. They took off of the main road, going up over the hill there into our place.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So there was a big snag. Oh, the snag was about

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: 25, 30 feet high. A great big snag that burned off in the fire. And oh, the tree was gone. Dang, it must have been three and a half feet through. So you see, it was quite a stump. So I said to my brother, I said, now I tell you what I think we better do. We better sneak down, I says, and stay behind this keep. I know the stump. Yeah. So we walked on down and I looked. And I said, oh, by Jiminy here, he's already, he's already went by here. He's up on the hill on the upper side. Well, I said, let's get down to the tree, that stump. So we got down there. And I just stepped around the tree there. I looked and I see him and he jumped up on a windfall. Well, this windfall was about that high off of the ground, a tree about that big through. He jumped up there because it was so brushy.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He didn't care to go through it, so he thought, well, I'll jump up on this log and go on to where there'd be a clearing, don't you see?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, after he got up on the log, and he made two or three, four steps, and then he stopped. I pulled up the old rifle and took aim and let him have it. I knocked him off of the log. And I tell you, that was the most pitiful noise I ever heard that bear. It sounded like a human being. A dying. By God, it's a fact. I bet you'd never shoot another one. Well, no, I would shoot another one. But they have such a pitiful, you know, when they're dying. It's just pitiful to listen to them, you know. It's just like a human being, you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What caliber rifle did you have?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I had a .30 -30.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Do they cry or?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: They feel something on the cry, just like a human crying. It sounded like a man's voice.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So we started to go on up there. He'd already fell off of the log there, you know, down in the brush. He was just kicking and doing his last kicking when we got to him.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: By God, that meat was just as pretty and red as anything he ever seen. I skinned him out.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you sell the hide then, or what?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I don't remember that. No, I didn't sell the hide. They just let the hide lay around there.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Prepped it wouldn't have been much good in the summer. Oh, yeah, the hide, I think, would have been all right. But I didn't want to fool with it. A lot of people have them for rugs, but I never cared for that type of thing. But that used to be popular here, you know, in the early days. Yeah, we had a big old bear, a rugged hombre.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, white and kind of gray looking, you know. Oh. A great big one, a mammoth thing. Hmm. And I was a kid.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you sort of take care of the, were you, did you help support the family at all, being the oldest brother?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, yes. We both done that. I mean, we worked out, and what we made off of the place, I had to help. We didn't have any money. Just what we got to work, what stock we sold.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Then you worked for the mill down there, too, didn't you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yeah, I went down there and fired in a sawmill there one summer.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When you were pretty young?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was about 16, 17.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was that down at Bowe? Huh? Was that at Potlatch, you went?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, no, that was down on the Potlatch Creek, all right. The fella named Hanson had a big mill. So they wanted a fireman, so they gave me a job of firing there. Oh, I've done other work in the mill. I've rode courage.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You know how they used to ride the carriage?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I've been to timber, too.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Have you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, you bet.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: In the sawmill?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: In the sawmill. Yeah, they had two men there, you know, on the carriage. Oh, yeah, I was saw here in a busy spruce mill. We used to cut all trees, we cut them.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Three big camps up there, about 225. And Camp 1, Camp 2 had about 250. No, let's see, Camp 2 had about 215.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And about, what, 200 and Camp 3?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did your father end up, did he always dislike living there?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: I got the feeling that if he had his way, he wouldn't have come out west.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did he?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, you know, the old man, I think, at Longbury stayed there. The more he liked the place, that's the way I figured it. But, you know, if something happened to the old man, too, he was taken away.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Who'd you heard about that?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No. Well, you know, after I went into the Army in 1917,

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: the old man, I guess he was thinking of killing somebody, he took the rifle, went to town one time.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Had a year of 1918, wasn't it?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: That's when the First World War. Yeah. There was one or two around there and he didn't kill him. Yeah, I got there. Some of them, by goodness' sake. But they seen what was happening, what was the matter with the old man. They got scared there in Louisville, you know. Hell, he took it. He went and even bought a box of cottages, I think, too. It's funny that they'd sell him a box of cottages. And then they'd start filling the magazine and put one in the barrel. And he was ready to go out and kill. So, thank God the Grob brothers, there's where the trouble started, was up at the Grob brothers.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He didn't get along with the Grob brothers?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, not too good. I don't know. I wasn't there when this happened and the kids don't know much about... Well, we wasn't there. They don't know much about it because he went to town and he didn't show up the next day. I was working on the ridge. I don't remember where you were.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Were you home that night?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was a very big gal. I was only about 11 then. Well, anyway, I was working for Mrs. Ingle a lot on the ridge.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was not at home when you were not home?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, I wasn't. When I got home, that's what I found out because they took me home after we got through I was with the harvest.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that so?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I went there cooking. Oh, I didn't know that. Yeah. So, he was sent to Orifino and that's what happened. They committed him. You know... He hadn't actually done anything. No, he didn't. Just because they thought he was getting queerer. He was kind of unbalanced, I think, but there's a lot of things that entered into that too. Dad was badly injured when he was just a boy at home in Minnesota. He was kicked by a horse and I saw our uncles, you know, be about 27 years ago, 26 years ago, and Dad was unconscious for I don't know how long.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And he pulled out of that and then he was hurt at home when he cut that tree, remember?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah, yeah. He made out there. He was... I remember that. That hit him suddenly. He had injury.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What happened that time? What happened? What happened to him that time? He was out sowing that tree down, remember?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: And Mama said he never came home. He was chopping the tree down. Yeah, and he was hit by a limb or something from there. No, that... You see, sometime a tree will burn if in a funny way. It'll burn a hole out. And it must be on account of a pitchy limb.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And it'll keep burning and all out and all you got is a little stripping around on the out edge of solid wood, don't you see?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, here you got this other top hanging up there and it's just hanging there, by goodness. And if you'd shake it, that thing would wobble just like that. Well, the old man, he didn't use good judgment. It's the only thing I can say. So he started and he was going to cut that gov 'n dang snag down. Well, this hole was way up to 20 feet up from the ground where that fire had burned out. And Jim, and he cut, you know, and he cut and cut, you know, and the gov 'n dang... Well, that shook that loose part on the top there from that burn. And pretty soon, the gov 'n dang, it happened to hit the end pretty hard with the axe and it broke off and it hit the old man right on top of the head. Well, not exactly. It hit him on the side of the head. And here, pretty soon, iron maw, we come out of the house and the old man come and the god, his face was just a mass of blood. God, it was terrible how... God, he says, I've been unconscious, he says, for an hour. Then he says, I didn't know, he says, what did they... It hit me, he says. The top of a tree, he says, fell down on me. And the old man had been laying there for quite a while before he come to. Then he come to, he come to the house. Well, you see, now that probably had effect on his mind too, you know. He never was, seemed like right after that. Well, he probably got a pretty fair concussion from that. Yeah, yeah. No, I figured out what happened to me. I got kicked with the horse with both feet. Yes, that's all.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The side of the head or the front?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: I was kicked right in the stomach, throwing right over into the other stall.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, you were... I hit me on the head, but I hit my head on the other thing when I landed. Oh. No, my dad was kicked with a horse. You know that.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: He's told you about it, didn't he?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, he never told me that. Yes, we're told as a matter. My uncle, Jack, we saw him in Minnesota, 26 years ago. I never heard my dad say anything about that. He was unconscious for quite a while.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Is that so?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yes. Well, the old man never told me that. I remember you telling me about it before, but the old man never did tell me about it. But he said he fell off of the wagon one time when he had a barrel. He was hauling water from the river. So the boys, they set up front there, and they told my dad to sit back there and hold that barrel, keeping from tipping over. It was kind of rough going, I guess, and he was running and driving the horses fast. Well, anyway, the goddamn barrel tipped over, and the old man fell out of the wagon, and the hind wheel of that wagon ran right over the middle of his back, right straight across. The old man, he said, he couldn't get up for quite a while. He just about almost broke his back in two. You know, he did have trouble with his back, too. You remember that. I remember it. He drove his back a lot of times with Linda when I was a little girl. I remember that so well. You know, I remember time. Well, he had some hard knocks. He had some hard knocks, and he's right. You know, these Clytail horses, that's what we had there. We had some of this. This Clytail, I got great big feet like that, and I walked in behind him to walk in and give him some oaks. And she just come like that, and they flew me right in. They put a stall right back there by the round bar, and I hit my head on it. It was big, heavy stuff, you know. I hit my head on that and came down, and I was sitting this way, and I was trying to get my breath in. I had to roll over, and I kissed, and I couldn't get my breath. I thought I was going to die. Yeah, I seen fellas being kicked by a horse. But you remember, now there's another story, too, that you can tell about when you got bucked off of that horse. Yeah. It got broken in three different places. Yeah.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What's that one? Was that the right arm or the left?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: The left. The left arm. They had to take him to Troy and Lyle. That was many, many, many.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And how was this? How did that happen?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, I got on the horse, and the horse started bucking, and they bucked me off, and back gully I fell. I don't know how I fell, but I fell and broke my arm. They broke it in one place. No, in two places, I think it was. I thought it was just in three. No, no, no, no, no, not three. Two places. A mistake, too. So, that was bad shocks I got over that, you know. In a month or so, it was always good as new. Yeah, but you had to drive. Dad had to take him to Troy, and that's how many months. Well, yeah, that was the only doctor down there. That was the closest doctor. That was the closest doctor. That was about 13, 14 miles from home.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He had to wait till daylight, didn't you?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, my gosh, yeah. Before he could do anything, because he had ether, and he couldn't do it. They didn't have electric lights, and gas, I guess. And they gave you chloroform. Chloroform, you can use ether. Chloroform, that's what it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: The doctor gave you chloroform, didn't he?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Put you to sleep and set your arm. He'd come home with us. Well, I'm glad they didn't break my neck.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: There was one of those Indian ponies, and she was awful tricky, you know.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: So, you and the baby, huh?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. You're still a baby. I went over and got me a whip and whipped it. You went over and run her up and down the hill and she went down the thing.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you ride a lot as a kid?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Oh, I used to been riding standing up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: And you were young?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Yeah. Oh, yeah. We got a showgirl. I liked to be in the circus. Climbing trees. She was a tomboy. And I was, you see, I'm about six years younger, so I missed out on that. And I was too young to do this, go that, go this, that, and the other thing. You go home. Yeah, I don't want you. You stay home. You're too little. Then when they water, they'd say, come, baby, come, baby, come, baby. Yep. That's the way it was.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Why don't you have quite a crowd over here? Huh? Do you have quite a crowd?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: You were here last night at the crowd. Ah.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Now it's working.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Now that we've said what I wanted to do, it's working.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was that trip like back to Minnesota?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Oh, yeah, it was a fine trip. Jimin rode on the train, got to see the engines.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Ah, yeah. Well, it was different, you know. The more she made a big lunch, enough to last us till we got there.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: But then let me see, what kind of a coffee pot?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: She had a coffee pot, and she had to get some coffee. Well, I think she had some coffee at the clown's salon. So she, they used to have these stoves in there, you know, in the coaches.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: You remember those stoves?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: No, they didn't have them now. But anyway, they had those stoves. You could go over there, you know, and put the coffee pot on there, and you got cold water there, you know, and you could make some coffee. Oh, we had pretty good, Shucks. We had, we were all made up of a lot of sandwiches, cookies, some donuts. And of course, I didn't drink much coffee them days, but more, she wanted her coffee, you know.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: Well, the conductor says, why help yourself?

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: There's a stove. He says, you can make all the coffee you want. So all my more, I went into the dining room on there, you know, where they have the dining rooms. I don't know what she went in to get that time. But you come back with something, I forget what in the dickens it was.

speaker: Alfred Erickson or Lena Justice or May LeMarr
words: But Jiminy, we was on the road there for about four days.
